did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes a multiclass machine learning model for dermatology lesion diagnosis (melanoma, nevus, and seborrheic keratosis) using dermoscopic images from the ISIC dataset. The method involves feature extraction using HOG, SIFT, GIST, and color features, followed by classification using Kernel Support Vector Machine (K-SVM) and Random Forest classifiers.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a multiclass machine learning model for lesion diagnosis using dermoscopic images from the ISIC dataset. It details specific methodology (feature extraction using HOG, SIFT, GIST, and color features; classification using K-SVM and Random Forest), describes the dataset split (900 training, 235 validation, 500 testing), and reports new experimental results and accuracy metrics in Tables 2 and 3. Although it includes a literature survey section, the primary contribution is the development and validation of a new diagnostic model, not a summary of prior literature.

title: Machine Learning Model for Multiclass Lesion Diagnoses
journal: Proceedings of NILES2020: 2nd Novel Intelligent and Leading Emerging Sciences Conference
brief_summary: This study proposes a multiclass machine learning model to diagnose three specific skin lesion types: melanoma, nevus, and seborrheic keratosis, using dermoscopic images from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) archive. The methodology involves a pipeline of filtering, feature extraction using HOG, SIFT, GIST, and color features, and classification via Kernel Support Vector Machine (K-SVM) and Random Forest algorithms. The dataset of 1,635 images was split into 900 for training, 235 for validation, and 500 for testing. Evaluation was conducted using K-Fold cross-validation accuracy, comparing feature combinations with and without K-PCA dimensionality reduction. The Random Forest classifier achieved the highest accuracy of 67.5% using a combination of GIST, Color, HOG, and 512 SIFT features, outperforming K-SVM. The authors conclude that Random Forest is superior for this task and suggest future work on ensemble models and additional features to address data quality issues.

lesion_types_studied.details: Classified as a malignant type. 685 images used in the ISIC dataset.
Classified as a benign type. 531 images used in the ISIC dataset.
Classified as a benign type but has some characteristics from the malignant type. 419 images used in the ISIC dataset.

all_task_types.target: Lesion diagnosis categories (Melanoma, Nevus, Seborrheic keratosis)
all_task_types.details: Multiclass classification of dermoscopic images into three discrete diagnostic categories: melanoma (malignant), nevus (benign), and seborrheic keratosis (benign). The model uses Kernel Support Vector Machine (K-SVM) and Random Forest classifiers to predict these discrete labels based on extracted features (HOG, SIFT, GIST, Color).

datasets_used.name: ISIC Dataset Archive
datasets_used.description: Dermoscopic images retrieved from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) archive. The paper notes that the official ISIC challenge dataset was unavailable, so images were retrieved from the site archive. The dataset contains labeled dermoscopic images of different diagnoses.

use.training: 900 images were used for training, divided equally among the three categories to avoid unbalancing.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To measure the performance (accuracy) of the proposed machine learning models (K-SVM and Random Forest) for multiclass lesion diagnosis.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using K-Fold cross-validation accuracy, and comparisons between models were made based on accuracy metrics without statistical significance testing.

without_feature_reduction.notes: Feat 2 with Random Forest achieved the highest accuracy (67.5%) among all experiments without feature reduction.

with_feature_reduction_K-PCA.notes: Results with K-PCA were lower than without feature reduction. Feat 3 with Random Forest scored the highest in this setting (63.5%).

ISIC Dataset Archive.overall_conclusion: Random Forest classifier outperformed K-SVM. The best result was 67.5% accuracy using Random Forest with features GIST, Color, HOG, and 512 SIFT features without dimensionality reduction.

architecture_used.main_model: Multiclass machine learning model using Kernel Support Vector Machine (K-SVM) and Random Forest classifiers

pipeline_phases: Filtering (median filter and morphological closing operations for hair removal)
feature_extraction_methods: Color features (Histogram in RGB and HSV formats)

training_details.hardware: MacBook Pro with 2.6 GHz Intel Core i5 processor, 8 GB RAM DDR3, no GPU

preprocessing_techniques: Morphological closing operations for hair removal
conclusions: The Random Forest classifier outperformed the Kernel Support Vector Machine (K-SVM) for multiclass lesion diagnosis.
The highest accuracy achieved was 67.5% using the Random Forest classifier with a combination of GIST, Color, HOG, and 512 SIFT features without dimensionality reduction.
Using 512 SIFT features yielded better accuracy than using 1024 SIFT features with the Random Forest classifier.

Applying Kernel Principal Component Analysis (K-PCA) for feature reduction resulted in lower accuracy scores compared to experiments without feature reduction, suggesting the dataset requires more features to represent the high similarity between images.
The overall classification results were not highly achieved due to the poor quality (high noise) of the dermoscopic images in the dataset.
Future work should focus on adding more features and utilizing ensemble models combining different classifiers to enhance performance.
important_remarks: The study is published in a conference proceedings (NILES2020) rather than a peer-reviewed journal, which may limit the rigor of the peer review process.

The dataset was retrieved from the ISIC archive because the official ISIC challenge dataset was unavailable, potentially introducing selection bias or data leakage risks compared to using the standardized challenge split.
The model achieved a low maximum accuracy of 67.5%, which the authors attribute to the 'poor quality (high noise)' of the dermoscopic images in the dataset.
The study lacks external validation; results are based solely on a single internal split of the ISIC archive data.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the performance differences between models or feature sets.
The hardware used for training was a standard laptop (Intel Core i5, 8GB RAM, no GPU), which limits the reproducibility of computational costs and potentially the scale of future experiments.

Feature reduction using K-PCA resulted in lower accuracy, suggesting the dataset requires more features to represent high similarity between images, which contradicts common practices of reducing dimensionality to prevent overfitting.

The paper does not report sensitivity, specificity, or AUC metrics, relying solely on accuracy, which may mask performance disparities across the imbalanced classes (Melanoma: 685, Nevus: 531, Seborrheic Keratosis: 419).

The study focuses on a multiclass problem (Melanoma, Nevus, Seborrheic Keratosis) but does not address the clinical challenge of distinguishing benign lesions from malignant ones with high precision, which is critical for dermatology.
The preprocessing steps (median filter, morphological closing) were applied to remove hair, but no details are provided on how other common artifacts (e.g., bubbles, ink markers, scale) were handled.
